finding.contract_claim: Parameter `error` is declared in the signature of log(message, error=False) (src/app/launcher.py line 1) as an accepted input that callers may pass.
finding.observed_behavior: The attributed call site, log("ok") in src/app/cli.py, does not pass `error`, so the parameter appears to always take its default False. `log` is a common name with high fan-out: src/noise.py defines an unrelated log(message) that is called locally.
finding.triage_reasoning: The claim frames `error` as a dead parameter because the only observed call site (`log("ok")` in cli.py) doesn't pass it. However, reality check: the parameter IS referenced in the function body — `return f"{message}:{error}"`. A parameter that contributes to the return value is not dead. The default `False` means callers that don't pass it get `"ok:False"` and callers that do pass `error=True` get `"ok:True"`. The fact that one call site uses the default doesn't make the parameter unreachable — it's reachable via any call that explicitly passes `error=True`. The `error` flag is part of the function's public contract and is actuated by the body. The reachability gap does not exist.
